figures.caption: Figure 4: Live-data proof of life on a DDS-generated stack. A Coinbase public WebSocket feed (20 USD pairs) is plugged into the running stack at T0. Top: cumulative raw trades (blue) and OHLCV 1-minute buckets (red); the red staircase is the ohlcv_1m materialized view emitting one row per (symbol, minute). Bottom: 10-second rolling ingest rate (green) tracks the exchange’s bursty traffic; end-to-end latency p50 (black) and p95 (dashed) stay bounded across the full window.
Table 2: Artifacts generated by a representative DDS run (T0, T1, T2 all pass).

Figure 5: Trimmed skills/clickhouse.yaml showing one entry per block (capabilities, operational, anti_patterns, compositions). Dated comments are real attribution-log entries from the learning-loop experiment.
Table 7: Per-run detail for the n=10 DDS one-shot configuration (no outer loop).
Figure 6: Trading-workload intent populating all six L1 dimensions (§3). The framework emits one soft warning (cost preference under-specified, defaulted to simplicity) and no hard errors.
Table 6: First-deployment failures on the trading backend and their layer attribution. All four observed at L4; three attribute to L3 skill gaps. The second deployment fixed 4/4 with skill patches.

Table 1: Runtime signals are typed and routed to the layer that owns the violated decision (cf. Fig. 3). Concrete signals from the trading case study and the skill patches that close them are in §4.3. Some signals (consumer lag, p99 violation) are genuinely ambiguous between L2 and L3; the harness accepts either label and the open problem of a confidence model is discussed in Appendix H.
Table 8: Per-run detail for the n=10 DDS + 5-iter outer-loop configuration. “First→T1” is the iteration in which T1 first passed (smaller is better; iteration 1 = no outer feedback needed).
